denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your provider submitted for a diagnostic breast ultrasound with diagnostic mammography to evaluate fibrocystic breast changes. After review under Cigna Medical Coverage Policy, the request is not authorized. Determination date: May 22, 2026.

The request is denied because a required peer-to-peer review was not completed within the allowed window. The timeframe for that peer-to-peer review was provided verbally by telephone to your provider's office and is not confirmed in this letter. Because the review was not completed within that window, the prior authorization cannot be approved at this stage.

Separately, on the records reviewed, the request did not meet the applicable coverage criteria. The criteria applied reflect breast-imaging screening guidance for women ages 50 to 74, and the submitted documentation did not establish that the request satisfies those thresholds.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific coverage criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
clinical_context: The patient is a 33-year-old woman with longstanding fibrocystic breast changes who now reports a new, focal, palpable lesion measuring about 1.4 cm in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, with associated localized pain that began in the first quarter of 2026. The finding is discrete on clinical breast examination and distinct from her baseline nodularity. Her primary care physician recommended targeted diagnostic breast ultrasound, with diagnostic mammography and possible cyst aspiration, to characterize the lesion; a new, focal, symptomatic finding warrants diagnostic imaging regardless of routine screening age. She has a first-degree family history of breast cancer in her mother, diagnosed in her late forties. The screening guidance for women ages 50 to 74 that the insurer applied governs asymptomatic screening, not the diagnostic evaluation of a new symptomatic lesion in a 33-year-old. Regarding the peer-to-peer review, the provider's office received only a verbal telephone notice of the review window and never received written confirmation of the timeframe, making it impossible to verify or meet; the office logged the call but could not confirm the deadline in writing.

Both flaws textbook-realistic (50-74 screening misapplied to symptomatic diagnostic work-up; verbal window not put in writing). Coherent demographics; current standard of care.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Procedurally, the P2P deadline was verbal-only and never confirmed in writing — an unmeetable, undocumented deadline. Clinically, the insurer applied ages 50-74 asymptomatic screening guidance to a symptomatic 33-year-old with a new 1.4 cm palpable lesion and first-degree family history. No genuine defense survives; easy appeal on both vectors.
